item.title: Ingmar Bergman
item.description: Movement: Scandinavian Existential Cinema Ingmar Bergman is cinema’s great interrogator of the soul—or what remains when the soul empties out.

item.title: (1957) Wild Strawberries
item.description: Original title: Smultronstället Director: Ingmar Bergman Movement: Scandinavian Existential Cinema Professor Isak Borg, seventy-eight years old, is driving from Stockholm to Lund to receive an honorary degree.

item.title: (1963) Winter Light
item.description: Original title: Nattvardsgästerna Director: Ingmar Bergman Movement: Scandinavian Existential Cinema Pastor Tomas Ericsson conducts communion in a nearly empty church in rural Sweden.

item.title: (1964) A Hard Day's Night (film)
item.description: Director: Richard Lester Influence: French New Wave Four musicians are chased by fans, ride a train, evade their manager, wander off, get into trouble, play a concert, and escape again.

item.title: Existential drift
item.description: Existential drift names a condition in which characters move through the world without stable goals, moral anchors, or narrative direction—experiencing life as a sequence of contingent moments rather than purposeful action.

item.title: Memory as structure in modernist cinema
item.description: Memory in modernist cinema operates not as content to be recalled but as a formal principle that organizes narrative, temporality, and perception.

item.title: Modernist alienation
item.description: Modernist alienation names the condition in which characters experience disconnection from others, from social life, and often from their own emotions or sense of meaning.

item.title: Spectacle and spectatorship
item.description: Spectacle names the condition in which experience becomes image — where events are produced, circulated, and consumed as performances rather than lived as encounters.
